F ern P ot-rrrcAt- P nacrlcEs C oMMrs s roN
P.O. Box 807 ..128 J Streer . Sacrumenro. CA 95gll-0g07
(916) 322-5660 o Far (916) -12l-0886
March 26,2002
Mark D. Hensley
Burke, Williams & Sorenson, LLp
6l l West Sixth Street, Suite 2500
Los Angeles, CA 90017-3102
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. .{-01-291
Dear Mr. Hensley:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of councilmember
Nancy wernick regarding provisions of the political Reform Act (the . Act,,j i
QUESTION
May councilmember Wemick vote on land use entitlements sought by a developer
with a contingent interest in real property owned by Federal Express Co-rporatio.r, a
business entity that employs the councilmember's irusband?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Wemick may not vote, or participate in any offrcial capacity, in
any decision relating to the developer,s applications.
FACTS
Nancy Wernick is a member of the city council of the City of El Segundo. Her
husband is employedby Federal Express Corporation, which owns some 45 acres of real , ,
property in El Segundo. A third parry developer has acquired a contingent interest in the
Federal Express property. The developer has agreed to iay approximaiely $45 million
for the Federal Express property, subject to the acquisition of various land use entitle-
ments from the City of El Segundo. The develop.i ir seeking a development agreement,
' ' ' " I borirn-.n, bodeseition, titobo- 910i4. commission i"luiotionr'op i"i, otirti{ii;;;i'i-d;--'-
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-01-291
Page No. 2
general plan and zoning code amendments, and a specific plan designation for the
property. The land use entitlements are subject to city council approval.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public offrcial from making,
participating in making, or in any way using his or hir offrcial position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a "financial interest." ($ 87100.) Section
87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the offrcial, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any of the offrcial's economic interests.
Re-g-ulation 18700(b) describes in detail the analytical process used to determine if
a public official has a conflict of interest in a particular decision. The El Segundo city
council is a local government agency ($ 82041) and, as a councilmember, Ivis. Wernick is
a public official within the meaning of $ 82048. Your request for advice presupposes that
she will make governmental decisions on the developer' j applications. Hurini aisposed
ofthese preliminaries, we advance to the third step of the analysis, identifying-the 
-
official's economic interests, which may be any of the following:
' 
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 2 of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation I 8703. I (a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, purt r"r,
trustee, .rpJgl:9, or holds any position of management (section s?torlay
Regulation I 8703. t(b));
' 
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (secrion 
-gzr-0:(u); 
Regulation
18703.2);
' 
A public offrcial has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prioito the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 g703.3);3
' 
A public offrcial has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months priorio the decision
(Section 87 I 03(e); Regulation tB7 B.g;
= An indirect inrestment or interest-means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an official or'
by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a pubtic offrciar, or Uy a business
endq'or tmst in rvhich the official, the offrcial's immediite family, or theirigents own aiecuy, indirectly,
or beneficiallv a lo-percent interest or greater. (section 87103.) 
- :'Immedia6 family'' is aerrnea at section
82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
] fakiriq inio aicount'vour c6mmuriiw property'interest inyour hurU*atir,"o*., you hr;; il;;d;----
interest iu a source of income rvlrich provides yo,r. husbani $ 1,000 or more in :rny iz month period.


File No. A-01-291
.oo';;;;;"**cinterestinhisorherpersonal.-o..,:,:u,:;:;
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family-this is the
"personal financial effects" rule (Section g7103; Regulation Is70i.5).
Councilmember Wernick has an economic interest in her husband's employer,
Federal Express Corporatioq under subdivision (d) of $ 37103. We also presume that,
by reason of her community property interest in her husband's income, this business
entity is a source of income to her within the meaning of $ 87103(c). A public offrcial
always has an economic interest in his or her personal expefftes, income, liabilities or
assets, and those of his or her immediate family (defined as the official's spouse and
dependent children, $ 82029). In light of our analysis of foreseeable effecis on Federal
Express Corporation, we need not reach the question of "personal financial effects."
After identifying the official's economic interests, we deterrrine whether the
councilmember's known economic interests are directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions at iszue. The subject of these decisions would be the developer's land use
applications and the underlying real property. The developer here pursues land use
entitlements for real property owned by another, pursuant to a salei agreement contingent
on the developer's acquisition of these entitlements. In such a case, the applicant acts as
the agent of the landowner, as well as on its own behalf Under regulation-187g4.1, the
landowner (here, Federal Express Corporation) is directly involved in decisions on these
land use applications.
When a business entity or source of income is directly involved in a decisioq the
effect of that decision is presumed to be material. That presumption may be rebuttei by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision wrllhave ory
financial effect on the business enttty. (Regulations 18704.1(b); 18705.1(b).) This case
involves a $45 million real property transfer, and you have not.pointed to'any facts
suggesting that the planned transaction would have no foreseeable financial "ff.ct 
oo
Federal Express Corporation. We conclude, accordingly, that decisions on these land use
applications would have a foreseeable, material financial effect on Federal Express
corporation, which is an economic interest of councilnember warnick.
Councilmember Warnick thus would have a conflict of interest in decisions on the
developer's applications, unless the "public generally" exception were applicable, or her
participation in decisionmaking were legally required. (Searegulations j'gzoz-rrizoa.;
You have not disclosed any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable here, and
we therefore conclude our analysis at this point.


FileNo. A-01-291
Page No. 4
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-s660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counselb-[(WM
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: May councilmember Wemick vote on land use entitlements sought by a developer
with a contingent interest in real property owned by Federal Express Co-rporatio.r, a
business entity that employs the councilmember's irusband?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Wemick may not vote, or participate in any offrcial capacity, in
any decision relating to the developer,s applications.
